name: Circular Route of Hornachuelos and Las Navas
name_original: Las Navas de la Concepción desde Hornachuelos — circular por el Parque Natural Sierra de Hornachuelos
description: Cycle a challenging 46.0 miles (74.0 km) route through Hornachuelos Sierra Natural Park, featuring Mediterranean forests and wildlife.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Hornachuelos are situated within the Sierra de Hornachuelos Natural Park, a region characterized by well-preserved Mediterranean forests, rolling hills, and winding roads. The landscape features a mix of holm oaks, cork trees, and wild olive trees, with flatter areas transitioning into dense scrubland. Significant rivers such as the Bembézar and Guadalora carve through the terrain, creating deep gorges and feeding reservoirs like the Embalse de la Breña and Hornachuelos Dam. This area offers diverse terrain for touring cyclists, from challenging climbs to scenic routes along riverbanks. Best touring cycling routes around Hornachuelos The most popular touring cycling route is Circular Route of Hornachuelos and Las Navas, a 46.0 miles (74.0 km) trail that takes 4 hours 47 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers extensive views across the Sierra de Hornachuelos Natural Park. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Embalse de la Breña from Hornachuelos — circular through the Sierra de Hornachuelos Natural Park, a difficult 42.0 miles (67.6 km) path. This route circles the Embalse de la Breña reservoir, showcasing the park's diverse Mediterranean ecosystem. Local touring cyclists also love the Bembézar Dam – Junction towards the Silver Mines loop from Hornachuelos, a 31.1 miles (50.0 km) trail leading through Mediterranean forests and along the Bembézar River, often completed in about 3 hours 14 minutes. Touring cycling around Hornachuelos is defined by rolling hills, Mediterranean forests, and scenic river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to challenging climbs. The routes in Hornachuelos are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.15 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 170 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Hornachuelos's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Hornachuelos?
A: There are over 40 touring cycling routes available around Hornachuelos, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels and preferences. You'll find everything from moderate paths to challenging climbs through the Sierra de Hornachuelos Natural Park.

Q: What makes touring cycling in Hornachuelos unique?
A: Touring cycling in Hornachuelos offers a unique experience due to its location within the Sierra de Hornachuelos Natural Park, a Biosphere Reserve. You'll ride through well-preserved Mediterranean forests, past rolling hills, and along winding roads with minimal traffic. The region is characterized by significant rivers like the Bembézar and Guadalora, which carve deep gorges and feed picturesque reservoirs, creating spectacular landscapes.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Hornachuelos?
A: The touring cycling routes in Hornachuelos are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.15 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, the scenic views of the Mediterranean ecosystem, and the rewarding challenges offered by the rolling hills and winding roads.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Hornachuelos?
A: The best time for touring cycling in Hornachuelos is typically during spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is milder and more pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter days of winter. This allows for more comfortable rides through the Mediterranean forests and along the river valleys.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes for beginners in Hornachuelos?
A: Yes, Hornachuelos offers 5 easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners. While many routes feature rolling hills, there are options designed for a more relaxed pace. For example, routes around the Hornachuelos Dam tend to have less elevation gain, providing a gentler introduction to the area's beauty.

Q: Where can I find more challenging touring cycling routes in Hornachuelos?
A: For challenging touring cycling routes, Hornachuelos has 21 difficult options. These routes often feature significant elevation gains and longer distances. Consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027021043/circular-route-of-hornachuelos-and-las-navas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Circular Route of Hornachuelos and Las Navas&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 74.0 km with over 1,000 meters of elevation, offering extensive views across the natural park. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027008435/embalse-de-la-brena-from-hornachuelos-circular-through-the-sierra-de-hornachuelos-natural-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de la Breña from Hornachuelos — circular through the Sierra de Hornachuelos Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;, a 67.6 km route circling the reservoir with substantial climbs.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes that are circular?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in Hornachuelos are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Popular circular routes include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027021043/circular-route-of-hornachuelos-and-las-navas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Circular Route of Hornachuelos and Las Navas&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027008435/embalse-de-la-brena-from-hornachuelos-circular-through-the-sierra-de-hornachuelos-natural-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de la Breña from Hornachuelos — circular through the Sierra de Hornachuelos Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;. These loops are ideal for exploring the diverse landscapes without needing return transport.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I expect to see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Along the touring cycling routes, you can expect to see stunning natural features and landmarks. The region is rich with rivers like the Bembézar and Guadalora, and impressive reservoirs such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5617701" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bembézar Dam&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/919273" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hornachuelos Dam&lt;/a&gt;. You might also encounter the serene &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7163453" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hotel Monasterio de San Francisco&lt;/a&gt;, or enjoy the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5340608" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Seminario de los Ángeles&lt;/a&gt;, especially from paths along the Bembézar River.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural attractions accessible by bike?
A: Yes, beyond the natural beauty, you can access several historical and cultural attractions by bike. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7163453" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hotel Monasterio de San Francisco&lt;/a&gt;, a former convent, is situated amidst the sierra and offers a glimpse into monastic life. While not directly on all touring routes, some gravel bike tours from Hornachuelos can even lead towards the impressive Castillo de Almodóvar del Río, known for its historical significance.

Q: What kind of terrain should I expect on touring cycling routes in Hornachuelos?
A: The terrain in Hornachuelos is characterized by rolling hills and winding roads, offering a mix of challenging climbs and scenic descents. You'll cycle through diverse landscapes including Mediterranean forests of holm oaks and cork trees, riverbank ecosystems, and areas with dense scrubland. While many roads are paved and suitable for touring bikes, some segments might be unpaved, offering options for gravel or mountain biking enthusiasts.

Q: How long do the touring cycling routes typically take to complete?
A: The duration of touring cycling routes in Hornachuelos varies significantly depending on the distance and elevation. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027021043/circular-route-of-hornachuelos-and-las-navas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Circular Route of Hornachuelos and Las Navas&lt;/a&gt; (74.0 km) typically takes around 4 hours 47 minutes, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20122369" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hornachuelos Dam loop from Paterna&lt;/a&gt; (31.4 km) can be completed in about 1 hour 55 minutes. Always check the estimated duration for your chosen route on komoot.

Q: Is it possible to combine touring cycling with other types of cycling, like gravel or mountain biking?
A: Yes, the varied terrain around Hornachuelos makes it possible to combine touring cycling with other disciplines. While many routes are paved and ideal for touring bikes, the region also features unpaved segments and trails suitable for gravel or mountain biking. This flexibility allows cyclists to explore different facets of the Sierra de Hornachuelos Natural Park, from smooth roads to more rugged paths.
tips: A small viewpoint where we can see the Bembézar River that feeds the reservoir of the same name. Hornachuelos is a town in the Sierra Morena, where we can get our water supply from one of the public fountains, before continuing on our route.

If you stop to rest in Las Navas, take a walk through the Plaza de España. There you will find a bar where you can recharge your batteries, besides there is a beautiful Fountain of the Lions and a fun monument by Manuel Carballido. Also look for the tile with olive trees, dedicated to the working rural woman.
Built in 1970, its large hydrographic basin is dominated by the typical Mediterranean forest of holm oaks and cork oaks. Here you can see cormorants, herons and various species of ducks.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This challenging touring bicycle route is approximately 74 kilometers long and typically takes around 4 hours and 48 minutes to complete. However, this duration can vary based on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery or points of interest.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Las Navas de la Concepción from Hornachuelos cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is classified as a 'Hard bike ride' with 'Very good fitness' required. While largely paved, it features relentless rolling terrain with numerous hills and passes. Some segments may be unpaved and difficult, potentially requiring you to dismount and push your bike. You'll encounter both compacted and uncompacted dirt tracks in sections.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you'll pass through the charming settlement of Las Navas de la Concepción and enjoy views of the Retortillo Reservoir. Other notable spots include the Convent of San Calixto, the Visitors Centre Huerta del Rey, and the historic town of Hornachuelos with its castle and old walled enclosure.

faqs.question: Are there any scenic viewpoints on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route offers wonderful, jaw-dropping views throughout the Hornachuelos Sierra Natural Park. A specific highlight is the Hornachuelos Viewpoint, providing excellent panoramic vistas of the surrounding Mediterranean forests and open pastures.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife can I expect to see in Hornachuelos Sierra Natural Park?
faqs.answer: The Hornachuelos Sierra Natural Park is a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve rich in biodiversity. You might spot wild boars, deer, and various birds of prey such as black vultures, golden eagles, and black storks, especially in the extensive holm and cork oak woodlands.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the challenging nature and the Mediterranean climate, spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) are generally the best times to cycle this route. Temperatures are milder, and the natural landscape is vibrant. Summers can be very hot, and winters can be cooler and wetter.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the cycling route in Hornachuelos?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas aren't detailed, Hornachuelos is a town with a rich heritage, and you should be able to find public parking within the town to begin your circular route. It's advisable to check local parking regulations upon arrival.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for cycling in Hornachuelos Sierra Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on established routes within the Hornachuelos Sierra Natural Park. However, always respect park regulations, stay on marked trails, and be mindful of wildlife and the natural environment.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are often allowed in natural parks, specific regulations for cycling with dogs on this particular route are not detailed. If you plan to bring your dog, it's best to keep them on a leash, especially due to the presence of wildlife and potential for unpaved sections, and to check local park rules beforehand.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. You will cycle through sections of the Sendero de Sierra Morena, the CHG - Embalse del Retortillo, Avenida de la Reina de los Ángeles, PR-A 396, and Sendero Los Ángeles.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or resupply along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through the settlement of Las Navas de la Concepción and culminates in Hornachuelos, both of which offer amenities where you can find food, drinks, and other supplies. These towns provide good opportunities for a break and to refuel.
